request.question: The 1977 drama film The creature (Spanish: La criatura ) and the 1975 film The Beast (French: "La Bête" ) feature what relationship?
request.answer: between a beast (monster) and a woman

title: Human Desire
Beast of Gévaudan
La criatura
This Man Must Die
La Bête (film)
Beauty and the Beast (2014 film)
Beauty and the Beast (1946 film)
Zémire et Azor
Beauty and the Beast (1987 film)
The Wandering Beast
sentences: Human Desire is a 1954 black-and-white film noir directed by Fritz Lang, starring Glenn Ford.
It is loosely based on the novel "La Bête humaine", by Émile Zola.
The story had been filmed twice before: "La Bête humaine" (1938) directed by Jean Renoir and "Die Bestie im Menschen" starring Ilka Grüning (1920).

The Beast of Gévaudan (French: "La Bête du Gévaudan" ; ] , Occitan: "La Bèstia de Gavaudan" ) is the historical name associated with the man-eating gray wolf, dog or wolfdog which terrorized the former province of Gévaudan (modern-day "département" of Lozère and part of Haute-Loire), in the Margeride Mountains in south-central France between 1764 and 1767.
The attacks, which covered an area stretching 90 by , were said to have been committed by a beast or beasts that had formidable teeth and immense tails according to contemporary eyewitnesses.
The creature (Spanish: La criatura ) is a 1977 drama film, directed by Eloy de la Iglesia.
The plot follows a respectable bourgeois housewife who chooses the love for her black German Shepherd over her relationship with her husband.
"La criatura" is notable, like Walerian Borowczyk's "La Bête" (1975) and Nagisa Oshima's "Max, Mon Amour" (1986), for openly involving zoophilia, then a novelty in Spanish Cinema.
This Man Must Die (French: Que la bête meure ), American title The Beast Must Die, is a 1969 French and Italian thriller film directed by Claude Chabrol.
The story is based on a 1938 novel by Cecil Day-Lewis, writing as Nicholas Blake, "The Beast Must Die".
The film had a total of 1,092,910 admissions in France.
The Beast (French: "La Bête" ) is a 1975 X rated French erotic fantasy horror film written, edited, and directed by Walerian Borowczyk.
Although sometimes compared with "Beauty and the Beast", there are no parallels in the plot except that it features the relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman.
The film was noted for its explicit sexual content upon its initial release.
It has become a cult film.
Beauty and the Beast (French: La Belle et la Bête ) is a 2014 Franco-German romantic fantasy film based on the traditional fairy tale of the same name by Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve.
Written by Christophe Gans and Sandra Vo-Anh and directed by Gans, the film stars Léa Seydoux as Belle and Vincent Cassel as the Beast.
Beauty and the Beast (French: La Belle et la Bête ) is a 1946 French romantic fantasy film directed by French poet and filmmaker Jean Cocteau.

Starring Josette Day as Belle and Jean Marais as the Beast, it is an adaptation of the 1757 story "Beauty and the Beast", written by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont and published as part of a fairy tale anthology.
Zémire et Azor ("Zémire and Azor") is an opéra comique, described as a "comédie-ballet mêlée de chants et de danses", in four acts by the Belgian composer André Grétry.
The French text was by Jean-François Marmontel based on "La Belle et la bête" ("Beauty and the Beast") by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont, and "Amour pour amour" by P. C. Nivelle de La Chaussé.
The opera includes the famous coloratura display piece "La Fauvette" in which the soprano imitates birdsong.

Beauty and the Beast (alternatively: Cannon Movie Tales: Beauty and the Beast in United States, Die Schöne und das Biest in West Germany, La Bella e la Bestia in Italy, La Belle et la Bête in France) is a 1987 American/Israeli musical film, part of the 1980 film series Cannon Movie Tales.
It is a contemporary version of the classic tale of Beauty and the Beast.
The movie was filmed entirely in Israel, and the taglines were: ""The monster they feared was the prince she loved"" and ""The classic fairy tale about seeing with your heart"".
The Wandering Beast (French:La bête errante) is a 1932 French drama film directed by Marco de Gastyne and starring Gabriel Gabrio, Maurice Maillot and Os-Ko-Mon.
The film is set in Alaska.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

"La criatura" is notable, like Walerian Borowczyk's "La Bête" (1975) and Nagisa Oshima's "Max, Mon Amour" (1986), for openly involving zoophilia, then a novelty in Spanish Cinema.

La Bête (film) | The Beast (French: "La Bête" ) is a 1975 X rated French erotic fantasy horror film written, edited, and directed by Walerian Borowczyk.  Although sometimes compared with "Beauty and the Beast", there are no parallels in the plot except that it features the relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman.  The film was noted for its explicit sexual content upon its initial release.  It has become a cult film.

La Bête Humaine (film) | La Bête Humaine (English: The Human Beast and Judas Was a Woman) is a 1938 French film directed by Jean Renoir, with cinematography by Curt Courant.  The picture features Jean Gabin and Simone Simon, and is loosely based on the novel of the same name by Émile Zola.

The Wandering Beast | The Wandering Beast (French:La bête errante) is a 1932 French drama film directed by Marco de Gastyne and starring Gabriel Gabrio, Maurice Maillot and Os-Ko-Mon.  The film is set in Alaska.

Starring Josette Day as Belle and Jean Marais as the Beast, it is an adaptation of the 1757 story "Beauty and the Beast", written by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont and published as part of a fairy tale anthology.

Beauty and the Beast (2014 film) | Beauty and the Beast (French: La Belle et la Bête ) is a 2014 Franco-German romantic fantasy film based on the traditional fairy tale of the same name by Gabrielle-Suzanne Barbot de Villeneuve.  Written by Christophe Gans and Sandra Vo-Anh and directed by Gans, the film stars Léa Seydoux as Belle and Vincent Cassel as the Beast.

Inju: The Beast in the Shadow | Inju: The Beast in the Shadow (French: Inju, la bête dans l'ombre) is a 2008 film by Barbet Schroeder.  The film stars Benoît Magimel and Lika Minamoto and was filmed on location in Tokyo.
hop1_titles: Inju: The Beast in the Shadow
response.summary_1:
- [1] La criatura (1977): plot involves a housewife's "love for her black German Shepherd" and is "notable... for openly involving zoophilia."  
- [2] La Bête (1975): "features the relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman" and was noted for "explicit sexual content."  
- Bridge entities: La criatura (1977) — passage [1]; La Bête (1975) — passage [2]. No ambiguity among candidates.  
- Missing/ambiguous facts: none affecting the question (both sources explicitly indicate a human–animal sexual relationship).  
- Final answer: Both films feature zoophilia — a sexual/romantic relationship between a human and an animal/beast (supported by [1] and [2]).  
- Next retrieval: none required.

La Bête à Maît' Belhomme | "La Bête à Maît' Belhomme" is a short story by French author Guy de Maupassant, published in 1885.
La Bête Noire (comics) | La Bête Noire is a comic book written by Croatian writer Darko Macan and illustrated by Serbian artist Milan Jovanović published in Novi Sad in 2002.

La Bête Humaine (film) | La Bête Humaine (English: The Human Beast and Judas Was a Woman) is a 1938 French film directed by Jean Renoir, with cinematography by Curt Courant.  The picture features Jean Gabin and Simone Simon, and is loosely based on the novel of the same name by Émile Zola.

La bête lumineuse | La bête lumineuse (English: "The Shimmering Beast") is a 1982 Canadian documentary film directed by Pierre Perrault, about a group of hunters who gather annually to hunt moose near Maniwaki, Quebec.  It was screened in the Un Certain Regard section at the 1983 Cannes Film Festival.
response.summary_2: Both films feature zoophilia — a sexual/romantic relationship between a human and an animal. La criatura (1977) follows a bourgeois housewife who chooses love for her black German Shepherd and is noted for openly involving zoophilia. La Bête (1975) depicts a sexual relationship between a beast (monster) and a woman and was noted for its explicit sexual content.
